items.q: Which direction does water move in the xylem, and what drives it?
items.correct: Upwards from roots to leaves — driven mainly by transpiration pull (evaporation at leaves creating tension on the water column).
items.misconception: Water moves up the xylem because roots pump it actively using ATP.
items.random_wrong: Water moves both directions in the xylem depending on the time of day.
items.why: Students assume an active 'pump' is needed; they don't realise evaporation at the top, combined with water's cohesion, pulls the whole column up.

items.q: What is transported in the phloem of a plant?
items.correct: Sugars (mainly sucrose) and other organic compounds, from sources (e.g. leaves) to sinks (e.g. roots, fruits).
items.misconception: Phloem transports water from roots to leaves.
items.random_wrong: Phloem transports oxygen to all parts of the plant.
items.why: Students mix up xylem (water) and phloem (sugar); they don't link the function with each tissue.

items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.
The answer examiners want is: Nucleus. “Membrane” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A small section of DNA controlling one feature is a…
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gene. “Chromosome” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Humans have 46 chromosomes per cell. Half from each parent — how many from one?
items.why: Exact answer: 23. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
